title: Job 6
subtitle: Job Replies to Eliphaz: My Complaint Is Just

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — "If Only My Anguish Could Be Weighed"

heb.paragraph: "If only my vexation (kaʿas) could be weighed and my misery placed on the scales" (v.2). Job demands that his pain be measured objectively. His complaint is not self-pity but a claim that his anguish is proportionate to his outcry. He is not overreacting.

"The arrows of the Almighty (Shaddai) are in me" (v.4). Job names God as the one attacking him. He uses the divine warrior image: Shaddai is an archer, and Job is the target. This is not the Satan’s work in Job’s understanding — it is God’s.

refs.note: "Your arrows have pierced me" — the same divine-archer image.
"He drew his bow and made me the target for his arrows" — Jeremiah’s parallel.

notes.note: "If only my anguish could be weighed." Job wants objective measurement. His pain, he claims, would outweigh the sand of the seas.
"The arrows of the Almighty are in me." Job identifies God as his attacker. He has no concept of the Satan’s role — he sees only God.
"If only God would be willing to crush me, to let loose his hand and cut me off!" Job longs for death as mercy. He wants God to finish the job.
"Then I would still have this consolation — that I had not denied the words of the Holy One." Even wishing for death, Job’s integrity holds. He has not cursed God.
Calvin: Job attributes his suffering to God, not to the Satan. This is theologically correct in one sense — God permitted it — but Job’s mistake is assuming God is hostile rather than testing.
Calvin: even in despair, Job clings to one thing: he has not denied God. This is the thread that holds through the entire book.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes "arrows of Shaddai" portrays God as a warrior. The poison imagery suggests the arrows are dipped — the suffering intensifies over time.
The NET: "Does a wild donkey bray when it has grass?" Animals don’t cry without cause. Neither does Job. His outcry is warranted.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the scales image is legal — Job wants his case weighed by an impartial standard. If his anguish were measured, the court would rule his complaint justified.
Clines: this is the crux of Job’s self-defence. Even at his lowest, he has not "denied the words of the Holy One." The Satan’s prediction has failed.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Job’s reply is a legal rebuttal. He does not accept Eliphaz’s diagnosis. Instead he presents evidence: the weight of his suffering (proportionality) and his preserved integrity (innocence). Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: Job’s first reply to Eliphaz. He does not engage the retribution principle directly but makes an emotional case: my suffering is heavier than sand (v.3), God’s arrows are in me (v.4), even animals don’t cry out without cause (v.5). The argument: my complaint is proportionate to my pain.

sections.header: Verses 14–30 — "My Brothers Are Undependable as Streams"

heb.paragraph: "My brothers have been as treacherous as a wadi (naḥal)" (v.15). A wadi is a seasonal streambed — a torrent in winter, bone-dry in summer. The image is devastating: when Job needed his friends most (summer, drought), they dried up. The water was there when he didn’t need it.

"Anyone who withholds kindness (ḥesed) from a friend forsakes the fear of the Almighty" (v.14). Job connects the friends’ failure to love with a failure to fear God. Pastoral cruelty is not just unhelpful — it is unfaithful.

refs.note: "You are to me like a deceptive brook, like a spring that fails" — Jeremiah uses the same wadi image for God himself.
"A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity" — the standard the friends fail to meet.

notes.note: "Anyone who withholds kindness from a friend forsakes the fear of the Almighty. But my brothers are as undependable as intermittent streams." The theological connection: failing a friend is failing God.
"Now you too have proved to be of no help." Direct. The friends offered theology when Job needed presence.
"Teach me, and I will be quiet; show me where I have been wrong." Job is not closed to correction. He demands specifics, not generalities.
"Look at me! Would I lie to your face? Is there any wickedness on my lips?" Job’s final appeal: read my face, hear my words, judge for yourselves.
Calvin: withholding kindness is connected to forsaking the fear of God. The friends’ theological error produces pastoral cruelty, and pastoral cruelty is a spiritual failure.
Calvin: Job’s openness to correction is important. He does not claim perfection — he claims innocence of the specific charge implied by his suffering.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET explains the wadi (naḥal) — a seasonal streambed that fills during rains but dries completely in summer heat. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

The NET: "caravans of Tema look" — Tema is in northwest Arabia. Real trade routes depended on water sources that sometimes failed. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the wadi image is the chapter’s masterpiece. Caravans from Tema and Sheba arrive at the wadi expecting water and find dust. They are "distressed" and "dismayed." The friends are that dry streambed.
Clines: Job’s willingness to be corrected is crucial. He is not defiant for defiance’s sake. He wants evidence, not assumptions.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Job cross-examines the witnesses. He challenges the friends to produce evidence: "Show me where I have been wrong" (v.24). They cannot, because there is no evidence. The prosecution’s case is circumstantial. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: Job turns from God to the friends. The wadi metaphor is drawn from desert geography: caravans depending on seasonal streams find nothing when they arrive. The friends are those empty streams. Job then challenges them: "Show me where I have been wrong" (v.24). He does not refuse correction — he refuses unsubstantiated accusation.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI refuse to touch it; such food makes me ill.ESVMy appetite refuses to touch them; they are as food that is loathsome to me.NIVIs tasteless food eaten without salt, or is there flavor in the sap of the mallow?ESVCan that which is tasteless be eaten without salt, or is there any taste in the juice of the mallow?

rec.text: ""If only my anguish could be weighed." Job wants objective measurement. His pain, he claims, would outweigh the sand of the seas."

rows.label: The Scales — 6:1–13
rows.text: "If my anguish could be weighed." Proportionate pain, proportionate protest.

rows.label: The Challenge — 6:22–30
rows.text: "Show me where I am wrong." Open to correction, closed to assumption.

hebtext.note: "If only my vexation (kaʿas) could be weighed and my misery placed on the scales" (v.2). Job demands that his pain be measured objectively. His complaint is not self-pity but a claim that his anguish is proportionate to his outcry. He is not overreacting.

"The arrows of the Almighty (Shaddai) are in me" (v.4). Job names God as the one attacking him. He uses the divine warrior image: Shaddai is an archer, and Job is the target. This is not the Satan’s work in Job’s understanding — it is God’s.

"My brothers have been as treacherous as a wadi (naḥal)" (v.15). A wadi is a seasonal streambed — a torrent in winter, bone-dry in summer. The image is devastating: when Job needed his friends most (summer, drought), they dried up. The water was there when he didn’t need it.

"Anyone who withholds kindness (ḥesed) from a friend forsakes the fear of the Almighty" (v.14). Job connects the friends’ failure to love with a failure to fear God. Pastoral cruelty is not just unhelpful — it is unfaithful.

thread.text: "Your arrows have pierced me" — the same divine-archer image.
"He drew his bow and made me the target for his arrows" — Jeremiah’s parallel.
"You are to me like a deceptive brook, like a spring that fails" — Jeremiah uses the same wadi image for God himself.
"A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity" — the standard the friends fail to meet.

themes.note: Justice peaks: Job demands proportionate judgment of his complaint and evidence from the friends. Faith holds: even in despair, Job has not denied God.
